one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-building survey that separates LLM 'content watermarking' from 'model fingerprinting', unifies model-watermarking and fingerprinting under intrinsic-vs-invasive categories, and is the first to survey fingerprint transfer, removal, and evaluation metrics as a model-lifecycle IP-protection problem.
key_finding: The central argument is that protecting LLM-generated CONTENT (text watermarking) and protecting the MODEL (fingerprinting) are distinct problems that the literature has conflated, and that most watermarking is insufficient for model attribution. Specifically: format/synonym/syntactic/neural-rewriting watermarks operate post-hoc on already-generated text and are fully decoupled from model weights, so they give zero model attribution if the pipeline is bypassed; logit- and sampling-based watermarks are inference-time interventions that vanish if an adversary has the weights but drops the decoding logic; only LEARNING-BASED watermarking (distilling/embedding watermark behavior into weights) can plausibly act as a model fingerprint, yet even those (per the cited Xu et al.) degrade quickly under continued fine-tuning and hurt harmlessness. The survey concludes that dedicated, purpose-built fingerprinting is needed, and that fingerprint transfer and removal are open, under-studied problems: fingerprints inherited via a base model are unreliable because embedding constraints can degrade downstream models, task gradients overwrite the signal ('fingerprint fading'), and inheritance gives no retroactive coverage of already-deployed derivatives.
core_contribution: A survey (not a new method or system) on copyright/IP protection for large language models. Its OWN contribution is conceptual and organizational rather than empirical: (1) it unifies previously fragmented terminology by folding 'model watermarking' into a broader 'model fingerprinting' umbrella, splitting it into 'intrinsic' (non-invasive) and 'invasive' fingerprinting, and coining hybrid terms such as 'backdoor watermark as fingerprint' and 'weight watermark as fingerprint'; (2) it draws a clear boundary between text watermarking (goal = trace generated CONTENT) and model fingerprinting (goal = attribute the MODEL itself), and argues that most text-watermarking schemes are decoupled from model weights and therefore cannot attribute a model once the watermarking pipeline is stripped; (3) it is, by the authors' claim, the first survey to systematically cover under-explored aspects of the model-fingerprinting lifecycle: fingerprint transfer, fingerprint removal, and standardized evaluation metrics; and (4) it proposes five evaluation criteria for fingerprints. The authors maintain a companion GitHub list (awesome-llm-copyright-protection). NOTE: virtually all concrete techniques named (KGW, HuRef, REEF, TRAP, IF, UTF, Chain&Hash, EmMark, etc.) are CITED prior work being organized, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Adversary/harm the surveyed field defends against: unauthorized use, redistribution, or re-licensing of a high-value proprietary LLM after it leaves the owner's control. Two concrete scenarios motivate the work: (1) Unauthorized model distribution/leakage of privately held models (insider leak of weights or exfiltration via cyberattack, then redistribution/monetization) -- the paper cites the Jan-2024 Mistral 'miqu-1-70b' leak on HuggingFace; (2) Violation of open-source license terms, where a third party makes minimal algorithmic changes to a released model and redistributes it (e.g., for commercial use) without required attribution -- the paper cites the 2024 Llama3-V vs MiniCPM-Llama3-V case. The defender is the model owner seeking to prove ownership/provenance. Fingerprints must survive downstream adversarial manipulation: fine-tuning, pruning, quantization, model merging, knowledge editing, decoding changes, and deliberate fingerprint-removal attacks; they must also resist 'false-claim' attacks where an adversary claims a fingerprint matches an unrelated model.
limitations: As a survey it contributes no new technique, no experiments, and no head-to-head empirical benchmark -- it standardizes five evaluation metrics (effectiveness, harmlessness, robustness, stealthiness, reliability) as qualitative design principles but does not measure the surveyed methods against them on common data, so relative rankings among methods are asserted from the primary papers rather than independently verified. The taxonomy is partly terminological (re-labeling 'model watermarking' as 'invasive fingerprinting'), which risks overlap/ambiguity at category boundaries the authors themselves note (e.g., learning-based watermarking straddling text-watermarking and fingerprinting). Coverage of some lifecycle areas (transfer via Fingerprint-Vector/LoRA-FP; removal via MEraser/TF/GRI) rests on only a handful of cited works, reflecting the field's immaturity. Robustness of essentially all fingerprinting families against a determined adaptive adversary remains an acknowledged open problem.

quantitative_results: Table 1 compares this survey against 8 representative prior surveys (Alkawaz & Salim 2017, Kamaruddin et al. 2018, Liu et al. 2024, Liang et al. 2024, Zhang et al. 2024, Lalai et al. 2025, Wang et al. 2025, Yang et al. 2025); the authors mark their survey as the only one comprehensively covering all of: text watermarking, invasive + intrinsic fingerprinting, transfer, removal, and metrics
Five fingerprint evaluation criteria are defined: effectiveness, harmlessness, robustness, stealthiness, reliability
Meral et al. (cited) identified 20+ grammar-informed syntactic transformation templates for Turkish watermarking
PREE (cited knowledge-editing fingerprint) is reported to maintain a negligible parameter offset of < 0.03%
No experiments or measured results are produced by this paper itself (it is a survey)
baselines_compared: Prior surveys used as comparison points in the coverage matrix (Table 1): Alkawaz & Salim [3], Kamaruddin et al. [68], Liu et al. [94], Liang et al. [93], Zhang et al. [203], Lalai et al. [78], Wang et al. [153], Yang et al. [185]
